text: US retail gasoline average prices have fallen nearly 10% since late May, down to $4.15 per gallon, the lowest since late April (it was $4.56 per gallon on May 26).

quotedTweet.text: INFLATION WATCH: With wholesale gasoline prices dropping today toward $3 per gallon, we should see US average gasoline retail prices falling  >10% in the next few days (from ~$4.5 to ~$4.0 per gallon). US average retail gasoline price has been >$4 for the last two months.

BRENT’s six-month calendar spread has almost halved as prices for nearby futures contracts fall much faster than for later-dated ones. The inflation-adjusted spread has averaged just under $11 per barrel so far in June (96th percentile for all months since 2010) down from a near-record of almost $20 in April. Traders increasingly expect significant tanker traffic will resume through the Strait of Hormuz soon to arrest the depletion of crude and fuel inventories before they become critically low:

Forward refining margins are back to the highs.

text:
Here’s a quick retro on my recent derivatives book. I’ve given back a massive chunk of gains over the last month. 

The July Brent contract and softs commodities some serious structural damage, though luckily riding $IBIT puts, long USD, and long copper helped absorb some of the bleeding. Of course it only offset a portion of it—it wasn't anywhere near enough

Equities were actually treating me pretty well, but Friday brought a massive VaR shock across the entire portfolio, and I'm still clawing my way back from that drawdown.

Currently, I’m layering into long dated Brent calls. Like we talked about, with near-term volatility spiking and liquidity completely evaporating, trying to trade prompt paper has become a total mug's game. 

So, I’ve built a position where I can just sit tight and let the clock run without getting chopped up.

Honestly, I’m completely out of sync with this current market and the r/r is garbage, so my game plan is to just sit on my hands and do absolutely nothing. 

Appreciate it, and good luck out there on the screens.

quotedTweet.text:
April’s winding down, and after all the chaos, the Jun Brent is finally expiring. 

I made another small donation with the profits from my pa. I’ve always felt being a little grateful brings good luck.

The world is just constant grim news lately, but I hope this helps a bit. Best of luck to everyone.

The EIA's latest STEO projects global oil demand will decline by an average 1.1 million barrels per day in 2026 before recovering by 2.5 million barrels per day next year. OPEC, who has so far refrained from making any major demand downgrades, will publish their update on Thursday, followed by IEA's updated market assessment on 17 June.
https://t.co/mf415auLs0 https://t.co/ZA7AzXcWGV
The FX Trader: USD rally faces CPI test and iron grip of long term range https://t.co/7Q7tyfz35B via @saxobank

CPI DAY IS HERE🚨

The May CPI report will be released today at 8:30 AM ET.

Here's what you need to know:

- Wall Street expects headline CPI to come in around 4.2% YoY, up from 3.8% previously.

- The increase is largely being driven by higher energy prices following the escalation in the Middle East and the renewed oil ( $WTI $CL $BRENT) shock. Core CPI is expected to remain much softer.

- The median forecast from major banks is 0.22% MoM and 2.8% YoY, suggesting underlying inflation pressures may still be relatively contained despite rising headline numbers.

Forecasts vary significantly:

- JP Morgan expects 0.27% Core CPI
- Goldman Sachs expects 0.17%
- Bank of America expects 0.20%
- Barclays expects 0.28%

The market reaction will likely depend more on Core CPI than the headline figure.

According to JP Morgan's trading desk, a Core CPI reading above 0.35% could send the S&P 500 ( $SPX) down 2% to 3%:

- 0.20%-0.25% Core CPI: Stocks could gain up to 1%
- Below 0.20% Core CPI: Relief rally potential of 1.5%-2%.

Oil remains the wild card. Several inflation models show the recent Hormuz-related oil spike pushing inflation higher in the coming months if energy prices remain elevated.

The last five CPI releases all produced volatility across equities, rates, and crypto, even when the data came in close to expectations. The timing couldn't be more important.

Markets are already dealing with one of the most volatile weeks of the year:

- The U.S. and Iran are openly exchanging strikes
- Asian markets are under pressure
- Tech stocks just experienced one of their largest intraday drawdowns in years.
- Crypto remains in fear territory

This means today's CPI report has the potential to either calm markets or add fuel to the current sell-off.

A hot inflation print could strengthen the case for higher-for-longer rates and put additional pressure on stocks ( $NVDA $TSLA $MU) and crypto ( $BTC $ETH $SOL $HYPE).

A cooler-than-expected print could give us the relief rally we have been waiting for.

quotedTweet.text:
GM

A LOT HAPPENED OVERNIGHT:

1. The biggest story remains the escalating conflict between the U.S. and Iran. After a U.S. Apache helicopter was reportedly shot down, American forces launched multiple waves of strikes targeting Iranian military infrastructure. Iran responded with threats and claimed retaliatory drone attacks against U.S. military assets across the region. Markets are now watching whether the conflict expands further.

2. Israel also continued strikes against Hezbollah-linked infrastructure in southern Lebanon. Ceasefire has failed again.

3. The Nasdaq ( $NQ $QQQ)experienced one of its largest intraday drawdowns in years, falling as much as 5.3% before buyers stepped in aggressively. Despite the panic, buyers managed to push the index well off its lows into the close, showing that dip-buying activity remains strong.

4. Asian markets are under pressure again this morning. Taiwan's equity market fell more than 2% as investors reacted to rising geopolitical risks and weakness across global technology stocks.

5. Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5, its latest generation of AI models. The release adds another major development to the AI race that continues to dominate market narratives.

6. Crypto markets remain under pressure. Total crypto market capitalization sits near $2.11T, down 2.1% over the last 24 hours. Fear & Greed remains deeply in Fear territory at 14/100.

7. Among the majors, $BTC is down 2.3% to $61.4K, $ETH is down 2.4% to $1,629, $BNB is down 1.8%, $SOL is down 2.4%, and $HYPE is one of the larger losers among major assets, falling more than 8% over the last 24 hours.

8. Despite the broader weakness, some tokens managed to outperform. WBT led with a gain of 13.7%, followed by BEAT (+15.5%), HASH (+9.8%), STABLE (+9.8%), and MORPHO (+8.8%). On the downside, SIREN collapsed 35.1%, LAB fell 26.6%, DEXE lost 11.2%, HYPE dropped 8.1%, and VVV declined 6.7%.

9. Investor sentiment continues to deteriorate. Nearly 44% of Americans now say they are financially worse off than a year ago, the highest reading since early 2023 and another sign that economic concerns remain elevated despite resilient markets.

10. Today's main event is CPI. Markets are already dealing with war headlines, extreme volatility in tech stocks, weakness across crypto, and growing macro uncertainty. If inflation comes in hotter than expected, it could add another layer of pressure and extend the current sell-off across risk assets.

THE NEXT 24 HOURS COULD SET THE TONE FOR THE REST OF THE WEEK.

🌐 Today's Pre-Market Brief by The Trading Analyst:

📈 Equity futures are pointing higher this morning as #semiconductor stocks continue to lead the market’s rebound following last week’s sharp pullback. The chip sector was a major source of strength on Monday and is once again helping drive positive sentiment ahead of the opening bell.

💻 Technology remains the market’s clear leader. While broader participation has been less impressive, continued strength in #AI and semiconductor names has helped keep the major averages near record territory. Investors remain selective, but the appetite for AI-related growth stories is still very much intact.

🛢️ Geopolitical concerns have eased after President Trump indicated that a deal with Iran could be reached within days. Reports suggest a proposed agreement would immediately reopen the Strait of Hormuz, helping push #WTI crude down 2.1% to around $89 per barrel and providing another tailwind for equities.

📊 Investors will be watching several economic updates today, including the April Trade Balance report and May Existing Home Sales data. Small business optimism also edged lower in May, highlighting some ongoing caution beneath the surface despite resilient economic activity.

🌏 #AsiaPacificMarkets finished mostly higher overnight, led by a strong rebound in South Korea as investors rotated back into technology shares. China’s latest trade data also came in stronger than expected, offering another sign that regional economic activity remains relatively resilient.

🇪🇺 #EuropeanMarkets are trading mostly higher as investors look ahead to this week’s #ECB meeting. Expectations remain high for another rate hike, while lower oil prices and improving risk sentiment continue to support equities across the region.

🚀 Market Wrap 09 JUN: Indices Rebound as West Asia Pauses Hostilities; PSU Banks & Midcaps Rally!

Indian benchmark indices bounced back into the green as cooling crude oil prices boosted market sentiment following a temporary halt in military strikes between Iran and Israel. Accompanied by positive news from the US-Iran peace talks, domestic benchmarks staged a broad-based recovery with heavily positive market breadth across the trading floor.

📊 The Numbers Game
Nifty 50: 23,242.10 (+0.5%)
Sensex: 73,918.76 (+0.5%)
Nifty 500: 22,370.15 (+0.9%)
Market Breadth: Highly Positive (1,862 Gainers vs. 821 Losers)
India VIX: Cooled down 8.6% to close at 15.6 points

🏛️ Macro & Sector Highlights
Mega Legal Relief for Telcos: The Bombay High Court quashed the government’s ₹24,000 crore one-time spectrum charge demands against Bharti Airtel and Vodafone Idea, declaring the operational notices legally unauthorized.

PSU Banks Surge: Nifty PSU Bank emerged as a top gaining index after the RBI released clear operational guidelines for its concessional forex swap facility to pull in foreign capital.

Top Performer: Transportation emerged as the best-performing sector of the day, gaining 2.2%.
Volume Pullers: Data Patterns (+10.1%) and Gabriel India (+7.5%) led the high-volume active gainers.

📢 Stock Specific Updates
✅ THE GAINERS
#PIIndustries: Jumped over 6% after its major overseas client, Kumiai Chemical, sharply raised its global H1CY26 operating profit forecast by 70.5%.

#PanaceaBiotec: Scaled an all-time high of ₹638 after joining the 10-partner global 'DENSTAR' consortium to clear regulatory pathways for its dengue vaccine in sub-Saharan Africa.

#GrasimIndustries: Advanced 1.5% on approving a ₹3,094 crore capex plan to install two new Cellulosic Staple Fibre manufacturing lines at its Karnataka unit.

#GodrejProperties: Gained traction after clocking over ₹2,000 crore in sales by selling 1,000+ residential homes at its newly launched Bengaluru project.
#JNKIndia: Hit a new 52-week high of ₹471.9 on securing an Abu Dhabi incinerator package order from CC7 Emirates for ADNOC's TA'ZIZ Salt Project.
#AdaniEnergy: Signed a definitive pact to fully acquire Intellismart Infrastructure for ₹3,050 crore to scale up its smart meter operations.
#StuddsAccessories: Exploded 15% following rigorous traffic safety enforcement and soaring helmet demand in West Bengal post-Assembly elections.

⚠️ THE LAGGARDS / NEGATIVES
#NLCIndia: Slid over 2% after the Government of India cleared an Offer for Sale (OFS) to offload up to a 3% stake in the company worth ₹1,263 crore.
#BluestoneJewellery: Declined as a large block deal saw a 3.8% equity stake worth ₹300 crore change hands via institutional sellers.

#GujaratFluorochemicals: Downgraded to 'Sell' from 'Accumulate' by Deven Choksey, citing pricing pressures and competitive headwinds from China.

🎯 Corporate & Institutional Actions
#TataMotors: Chairman N. Chandrasekaran highlighted that the impending acquisition of Iveco Group’s commercial assets will bolster the auto major's global expansion despite near-term macro cost pressures.
#BrigadeEnterprises: Board of directors approved a 3-for-1 bonus share issue and fixed the execution record date as June 18.

#CeraSanitaryware: Rose sharply as Goldman Sachs India Equity Portfolio picked up 1.2 lakh shares worth ₹64.9 crore via a bulk deal.

Overbought Radar: Aditya Infotech and Tata Technologies are actively flashing inside overbought technical territory.

#Nifty #Sensex #TelecomRelief #PSUBanks #GrasimCapex #PanaceaBiotec #PIIndustries #TradingUpdate

🐻 Market Wrap 08 JUN: Indices Plunge 1% as Crude Spikes Amid Israel-Iran Conflict; IT & Metal Stocks Drag!

Indian benchmark indices witnessed sharp distribution, closing lower by a full percentage point as global crude oil prices surged following a fresh round of airstrikes between Israel and Iran. A brutal combination of tech profit-booking, persistent geopolitical friction, and higher-for-longer US interest rate fears deeply unnerved local investors, leading to a highly negative market breadth.

📊 The Numbers Game
Nifty 50: 23,123.00 (-1.0%)
Sensex: 73,524.26 (-1.0%)
Nifty 500: 22,173.70 (-1.3%)
Market Breadth: Highly Negative (578 Gainers vs. 2,114 Losers)
India VIX: Jumped 7.9% to close at 17.0 points

🏛️ Macro & Global News
Geopolitical Gridlock: While the White House maintained that the fresh West Asian military strikes would not derail its indirect peace efforts, Iranian officials directly counter-stated that an agreement is "no longer feasible at this stage".

Record-Breaking Vehicle Retail: FADA data revealed India’s aggregate vehicle retail registrations jumped 9.6% YoY to 25.3 lakh units in May, driven by robust rural demand. Passenger vehicles comfortably spearheaded the momentum, climbing 23.3% to 4 lakh units.

Tech Sector Contraction: Following a steep 5% correction in the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100, local AI and data center infrastructure names faced severe valuation heat. Concurrently, the Nifty IT index marked a painful 9% drop over four days on brewing anxieties surrounding Generative AI's long-term displacement of standard IT delivery models.
Metal Downturn: The Nifty Metal index fell for its fourth straight session as stronger-than-expected US jobs data signaled prolonged monetary tightening by the Federal Reserve, threatening industrial material demand.

🏗️ Sector Highlights
Worst Performer: Telecommunications Equipment emerged as the hardest-hit sectoral pocket, plunging 4.4%.

Top Defensives: Healthcare and Pharma indices bucked the bleeding layout, tracking proposed non-PLI government support frameworks for bulk drug capacity and R&D.

Volume Shockers: Cera Sanitaryware (26.0x weekly average) and Syngene International (14.1x) experienced massive localized trading volumes.

📢 Stock Specific Updates
✅ THE GAINERS
#AlembicPharma: Closed in the green after striking a strategic joint venture with Difgen Holdings to develop and commercialize pharmaceutical solutions in Canada.

#KNRConstructions: Its 51% joint venture, KNR-SIML, bagged a massive ₹3,361.1 crore coal mining contract from South Eastern Coalfields for a project in Chhattisgarh.

#DEEDevelopment: Hit its 5% upper circuit limit after recording a solid ₹631.9 crore order inflow in May, pushing its aggregate book value to ₹2,433.9 crore.

#AdaniPorts: Its marine unit formed a joint venture with Argentina's Meridian Group to clinch a 10-year marine services contract for Argentina's premiere LNG export project.

#NRBBearings: Surged to a lifetime high of ₹422.8 per share after a heavy block deal saw 4.5% of the company's equity change hands.
#Ixigo (#LeTravenues): Acquired a dominant 54.7% stake in hotel booking platform Brevistay Hospitality for ₹65.7 crore, with an option to fully absorb the company later.

⚠️ THE LAGGARDS / NEGATIVES
#Wipro: Led the large-cap underperformers, shedding 8.4% as global IT sentiment curdled.
#SterliteTech: Hit its 5% lower circuit on profit booking from the global tech rout, though the optical fiber manufacturer remains up over 470% in 2026.
#InterGlobeAviation (IndiGo): Slid under severe pressure from rising crude overheads and reports that Airbus might stall imminent A321XLR deliveries due to regional supply chain strains.

🎯 Corporate & Capital Actions
#CarlsbergIndiaIPO: The Danish brewer is reportedly drafting papers for a $700 million initial public offering of its Indian unit—which commands a 22% market share—via a secondary share sale.
#ZeeEntertainment: Announced an upcoming board meeting on June 10 to clear a fresh capital raise via equity dilution or convertible security instruments.
#GujaratThemis: Board of directors approved a ₹1,000 crore fundraise via the Qualified Institutional Placement (QIP) or preferential share routes.

#HGInfra: Offloaded a 49% stake in its Raipur Visakhapatnam highway SPV to Neo Infra Income Opportunities Fund for ₹377.4 crore.

Overbought Radar (RSI): Electronic hardware major Aditya Infotech continues to sit inside highly overbought technical bands.

#Nifty #Sensex

text:
J.P. Morgan's Nifty 2026 Outlook: The Full Picture - 

Several reports are highlighting J.P. Morgan's 20500 Nifty target, creating unnecessary #panic. But here's what the report actually says -

Revised Nifty 50 Targets ( Year End 2026 )

Bull Case: 30000 ( Earlier: 33000 )

Base Case: 27000 ( Earlier: 30000 )

Bear Case: 20500 ( Earlier: 24000 )

The widely discussed 20500 target is not J.P. Morgan's primary forecast. It represents a #worst case scenario driven by -

A prolonged oil price shock

Disruptions in the Strait of #Hormuz

Earnings downgrade across sectors

Weakening market sentiment

Why did J.P. Morgan turn #cautious ?

Indian equities continue to trade at a premium to Emerging Markets.

Earnings growth expectations have been lowered.

Higher crude oil prices could hurt margins and consumption.

Limited #AI and #semiconductor exposure in Indian large caps.

Heavy supply from #IPOs, QIPs, and #promoter stake sales.

Bottom Line - 

J.P. Morgan's base case remains 27000, while 20500 is merely a downside risk scenario. Despite near-term concerns, the firm continues to believe in India's long-term structural growth story.

Bear Case ≠ Base Case.

#Nifty50 #IndianMarkets #StockMarket #Investing #JPMorgan

Daily Digest-6/9

📷DOW NASDAQ S&P 500 RUSSELL 2000

US stocks ended mixed as renewed tech and chip weakness and muted oil moves under geopolitical strain set cautious tone ahead of CPI data

S&P 500 (SPY) closed modestly lower, Nasdaq (QQQ) fell more sharply, and Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA) eked out a gain, reflecting rotation away from tech into industrials and defensives

Semiconductor stocks reversed an early rebound, with iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX) and PHLX Semiconductor Index both declining as investors continued to move out of overheated AI-linked names
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) fell during the session and dropped further after-hours after announcing a $7 billion equity financing that overshadowed its strong AI server backlog
Marvell Technology (MRVL), Micron Technology (MU), Broadcom (AVGO), Apple (AAPL), Salesforce (CRM), AMD (AMD), and Cisco (CSCO) all weakened, contributing to pressure on the information technology sector
Dow strength was supported by gains in Home Depot (HD), Sherwin-Williams (SHW), Nike (NKE), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Visa (V), JPMorgan (JPM), and other industrials, financials, and consumer staples names
Nine of eleven S&P 500 (SPY) sectors advanced, led by financials, materials, and consumer staples, while information technology and energy lagged
Oil (@CL) prices were muted despite US-Iran tensions, reports of a downed US helicopter, and comments from Donald Trump on a potential response to Iran
US trade data showed a narrower April deficit, but the release had limited market impact amid ongoing inflation concerns
J.M. Smucker (SJM) rose after-hours after beating earnings estimates as investors looked ahead to Wednesday’s CPI report and additional earnings for direction

📰 每日必读 | 6月9日

📊 宏观数据
美国2年期国债收益率突破 **4.1%**，市场开始押注美联储最早 **10月** 加息。穆迪首席经济学家警告美国经济正濒临衰退边缘，加拿大破产数量创下 **2009年以来** 新高。摩根大通高管称通胀若持续高位将带来风险，同时对存款代币表示零需求。

🌍 地缘政治
美国副总统万斯表示伊朗战争不会变成长期"泥潭"，但特朗普坚持美国处于伊朗协议"最后阶段"。与此同时，以色列继续猛轰南黎巴嫩，巴以局势持续升级。金融时报评论关注特朗普是否会在台湾问题上改变立场。

🏛️ 政策动态
美联储候选人沃什（Warsh）通往美联储主席席位的道路狭窄，市场对政策连续性存疑。众议员哈德森澄清特朗普中期选举言论被断章取义，众议院议长约翰逊将与总统会面。

💻 科技与AI
博通、阿波罗、黑石以 **350亿美元** 合作平台助力AI公司Anthropic算力扩张，高盛发布AI关键议题报告聚焦内存、货币化与估值倍数。SpaceX IPO引发特斯拉合并 speculation，部分投资者将其视为TSLA核心投资逻辑。代币价格因行业对成本激增的抵制而集体下跌。

🎯 交易员关注
• 地缘避险：关注黄金、原油波动，伊朗局势是关键变量
• AI产业链：算力需求端逻辑未改，关注并购整合机会
• 利率方向：关注美债拍卖与联储信号，2年期收益率是关键锚点

#每日必读 #市场速递 #投资参考

text:
过去 2 小时跟踪了全网 192 条新闻消息，筛出 5 条关注的核心信号。Trump 报告美军阿帕奇直升机在霍尔木兹海峡被击落并承诺反击，瞬间逆转了原油跌势，而 EIA 警告该海峡封锁将持续至 2027 年。

1. 地缘政治风险溢价瞬间重回。特朗普称伊朗在霍尔木兹海峡击落一架美军阿帕奇，美方必然作出回应。配合以色列国防军准备对伊朗进行更大规模打击的表态，中东局势已从外交博弈转回物理对抗。 $CVX $PLTR

2. EIA 发布结构性供应警告。经合组织石油库存预计将降至 2003 年以来最低水平，且霍尔木兹海峡航运预计要到 2027 年初才能恢复。这种长达三年的物理供应中断预期，将迫使市场对能源安全进行永久性重定价。 $URA $GEV

3. 半导体与高估值科技股遭遇重挫。费城半导体指数跌超 6%，$ARM 和 $QCOM 跌幅近 10%。三年期美债拍卖利率攀升至 4.19%，利率长期高企的压力正在加速挤出 AI 硬件领域的拥挤头寸。 $TSM $AVGO

4. 企业级 AI 转向自主代理范式。摩根大通计划年底部署可连续运行数小时的自主 AI 代理，Anthropic 发布 Mythos 5 模型并显著降低 token 消耗成本。AI 从简单的问答工具变为能够处理复杂软件工作流的数字员工。 $NOW $SNPS

5. 美国贸易结构向高价值出口转型。美国贸易代表访问 $AMAT 与 $TSLA，4 月贸易数据显示资本货物出口增加 40 亿美元。美国正利用半导体设备与能源优势缩小逆差，显示出其在全球产业链上游的控制力正在增强。 $AMAT $TSLA

那便是此窗口内的核心信号。若霍尔木兹封锁真如 EIA 预测持续到 2027 年，全球能源供应链还有多少冗余空间？觉得有用请转发。

As of 1:20 p.m. Beijing time, #Brent crude traded at $91.72 per barrel (+0.30%), while #WTI rose to $88.48 per barrel (+0.32%).

WTI crude sliding back to the $88 handle following confirmation that Strait of Hormuz ship traffic is recovering is giving equity desks a temporary sigh of relief, but do not confuse a tactical pullback with structural de-escalation.

The real story isn't just current spot barrel prices; it's the institutionalization of risk. The Reserve Bank of Australia’s mid-2026 financial stability brief hit the nail on the head: geopolitical choking points are no longer transient volatility spikes—they are shifting cross-border capital flows, ballooning war-risk insurance premiums, and altering corporate supply chains permanently. Smart capital isn't trading the day-to-day noise; it's positioning for a structurally higher floor in hard assets and shipping logistics. 🛢️ #Energy #Trading #Commodities

🔴 CPI DAY — and this might be the most important inflation print of 2026.

May CPI drops at 4:30 PM GST. Here is why Wall Street is nervous:

📌 Headline CPI expected: 4.2% YoY — a THREE-YEAR HIGH (up from 3.3% in April)
📌 Monthly: +0.5% MoM
📌 Core CPI expected: 2.9% YoY — moving in the WRONG direction for the Fed
📌 April PPI: 6.0% YoY with energy at 22.7% — businesses are passing costs to consumers
📌 WTI crude up 62% YTD — the primary driver of the inflation spike
📌 Survey of Professional Forecasters: Q2 inflation projected to hit 6%
What makes this release different from every other CPI:
1️⃣ It is the LAST inflation print before the June 16–17 FOMC — Warsh's first meeting as Fed Chair
2️⃣ ECB rate decision is TOMORROW (June 11) — back-to-back volatility
3️⃣ CME FedWatch is now pricing possible rate HIKES — not cuts
The scenarios:
📈 CPI above 4.2% → rate hike fears spike → USD rallies hard, Gold drops, equities sell off
📊 CPI at 4.2% (in-line) → priced in → brief volatility, then markets focus on FOMC
📉 CPI below 4.0% → relief rally → Gold up, USD down, equities surge
This is the number that decides whether the Fed cuts, holds, or hikes for the rest of 2026.

🇮🇷🇺🇸🇸🇦 The First Order Consequence: 
- Primary actor: @FirstSquawk (posting) claims: “Shell CEO SAWAN: oil market 1.2B barrels ‘in the hole’ from Hormuz.” 
- Primary actor: Shell CEO “SAWAN” alleged statement: a supply/demand gap described as “1.2B barrels” tied to the Strait of Hormuz
- Immediate consequence (for Shell and market participants): if credible, it implies a large near-term physical or financial tightness narrative, likely to raise crude price expectations and volatility
- Immediate unknowns that block falsification right now: 
 - Whether the quoted figure “1.2B barrels” is monthly, cumulative, incremental disruption, or an accounting net (receipts vs. shipments vs. inventories)
 - Whether “from Hormuz” refers to actual production offline, shipping capacity constrained, or inventory “in the hole” due to delayed transit
 - Whether “SAWAN” is accurately identified (full name/title) and whether the quote is verbatim, paraphrased, or attributed correctly
 - Whether the statement is speculative, forward-looking, or describing already-realized losses

🇮🇷🇺🇸🇸🇦 The Second Order Consequence: 
- Secondary/tertiary related actors likely implicated if the claim is treated as true (not confirmed by the text): 
 - Iranian-linked shipping risk, insurers, and charter rates: constrained routing around Hormuz can propagate into higher freight and insurance costs
 - Oil majors/traders: may accelerate hedging, re-route flows, and rebalance cargo allocation, potentially changing regional differentials
 - Governments and regulators: could face renewed pressure for maritime de-escalation, sanctions enforcement, or contingency planning
- Market-structure consequences if the “1.2B barrels” narrative gains traction: 
 - More persistent backwardation/contango shifts as traders reprice expected physical availability
 - Higher probability of retaliatory or preemptive commercial actions (stock draws, strategic inventory releases, contract renegotiations)
- Key uncertainties that could reverse direction (and thus growth/decay outcomes): 
 - Scale/time horizon uncertainty: “1.2B barrels” could be small in velocity terms (e.g., longer-run cumulative) or enormous in short-run terms; market impact depends on timing
 - Verification uncertainty: without a primary source (interview transcript, regulator filing, earnings call deck), the market may discount or overreact
 - Causality uncertainty: “from Hormuz” could be an attribution error (e.g., broader Middle East balance) rather than Hormuz-specific disruption

🕵️ monocle Discernment: 
- Historical training-data priors (general, not event-specific): 
 - Strait of Hormuz disruptions and shipping-risk headlines have repeatedly produced rapid crude volatility, insurance/risk-premium repricing, and rerouting costs
 - Large “barrels in the hole” figures are often framed with accounting language (expected vs. realized flows) and frequently require clarifying the time basis
- What is known from the input text: 
 - A named attribution exists: “Shell CEO SAWAN.” 
 - A numeric claim exists: “1.2B barrels.” 
 - A geographic attribution exists: “from Hormuz.” 
 - A mechanism phrase exists: “in the hole,” suggesting missing supply/flow or inventory shortfall
- What is not known (and is decisive for judging credibility and magnitude): 
 - The provenance: where and when the “SAWAN” quote was delivered (earnings call, conference, interview) and whether a transcript/record exists
 - The definition: whether “1.2B barrels” corresponds to physical barrels, net shipping units, or a modeled estimate
 - The timeframe: next quarter, next month, or multi-year cumulative gap
 - Consistency checks: whether other majors, analysts, or official shipping/inventory data corroborate the scale

🧠 Reasoning: 
- If the claim is a credible, time-bounded physical shortfall, then traders should reprice supply tightness quickly, benefiting actors positioned for higher spreads (refiners with flexibility, sellers of optionality, sellers of risk premium) while harming actors exposed to higher input costs or constrained throughput
- If the claim is an accounting/modeling estimate with a long timeframe, the immediate impact should be muted, and the “growth/decay” effect shifts toward sentiment/volatility rather than sustained fundamentals
- The biggest determinant right now is falsifiability: without a timestamped primary citation (transcript, recording, or statement link), the market’s reaction may decouple from fundamentals, creating short-lived price spikes and downstream uncertainty for planning

⚖️ Judgement: 
- Near-term likely: increased crude-price volatility and risk premium repricing driven by the combination of a specific named CEO attribution and a very large figure
- Confidence is constrained by missing verification details (exact identity of “SAWAN,” verbatim quote, timeframe, and definition of “in the hole”)
- Net growth/decay for the broader market positioning: likely short-term growth for holders of pricing power and hedging counterparties, with decay concentrated in cost-exposed refiners and contract-constrained buyers... until clarified by primary-source evidence

🟡 The Output Confidence Level: 5.1 out of 10.0

🛢️ Oil Rekindles Rate Hike Fears
Energy markets regained attention after recent friction between Washington and Tehran pushed oil prices higher and revived concerns about inflation. With Treasury yields climbing and expectations for restrictive central bank policy remaining firmly in place, commodities faced another session shaped by interest rate expectations rather than growth optimism.

🪙 Silver (XAGUSD): Slid toward $64 and touched its weakest levels in more than two months. Expectations for tighter monetary policy left silver vulnerable.

🛢️ Brent Crude: Climbed back above $92 per barrel as lower US crude inventories added support, helping oil recover part of its losses. #ZitaPlus #Commodities #Gold #Silver #BrentCrude

Economic War Diary - June 10 🗓️

Key Updates📰

1- US launches strikes on Iran after Apache helicopter downed🇺🇸
The US Central Command said it carried out what it described as “self-defense” strikes against targets inside Iran in response to the downing of a US Apache attack helicopter during patrol operations near the Strait of Hormuz. The military said the operation was a “proportionate response to unprovoked Iranian aggression.” US President Donald Trump defended the strikes, saying: “There were two pilots involved, both are safe and uninjured,” Trump said. “Nevertheless, the US must, of necessity, respond to this attack.”

2- World's third-largest container shipper warns against assuming Hormuz will return to normal🚢
Rodolphe Saadé, CEO of French shipping group CMA CGM, the world's third-largest container shipping company, said it would be “unwise” to assume conditions in the Strait of Hormuz will return to pre-war levels. He added that the company may continue offering alternative routes to customers, noting that shipping volumes to the Gulf have fallen to about one-third of pre-war levels. Alternative routes could cost the company an additional $300 million during the first half of the year.

3- Saudi jet fuel exports to Europe exceed pre-Hormuz closure levels ⛽
Data from Kpler and Vortexa showed Saudi Arabia is on track to supply Europe with more jet fuel in June than before the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, using Yanbu port on the Red Sea. EU and UK imports of jet fuel from Yanbu ranged between 118,000 and 140,000 barrels per day during the first week of June, the highest level since August 2025.

4- US energy secretary says oil exports through Hormuz and Gulf will keep rising 🛢️
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright said shipping activity and oil exports through the Strait of Hormuz have increased “very significantly,” adding that exports through Hormuz and the Gulf have risen and “will continue to rise.” He noted that restoring normal energy flows could take several months even after the war ends.

5- US oil exports hit record high 📈
US crude oil exports surged to a record 5.6 million barrels per day in May as disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz prompted global buyers to seek alternative supply sources, according to S&P Global Commodities at Sea data. South Korea emerged as the largest single destination, receiving a record 1.1 million barrels per day.

6- US gasoline inventories near minimum operating levels ⛽
US gasoline inventories are approaching minimum operational levels, which could weigh on exports during the summer driving season and push refining margins higher, according to S&P Global Energy CERA.

7- API reports 9.1 million-barrel decline in US crude inventories 📉
Data from the American Petroleum Institute (API) showed US crude oil inventories fell by 9.1 million barrels last week to their lowest level in four months.

8- US airline fuel costs jump 78% ✈️
Fuel expenses for US airlines rose 78% in April to around $6.5 billion, according to the US Department of Transportation.

9- Rystad Energy: Gas shortage could drive 70 million tons of additional coal use in Asia🔥
Rystad Energy forecast that Asia could consume an additional 70 million tons of coal in 2026 if gas market tightness persists, citing higher coal imports by South Korea and Japan and increased reliance on coal-fired power plants.

10- India's fuel demand hit by supply disruptions 🇮🇳
India's total fuel consumption fell 6.5% year-on-year in May, while demand for liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) dropped 20% amid higher prices and supply disruptions.

11- Record orders for very large crude carriers 🚢
Orders for very large crude carriers (VLCCs) currently stand at 262 vessels, the highest level since 2011 and equivalent to roughly a quarter of the existing global fleet, raising concerns about future oversupply in the sector.

12- Vale says global demand for minerals remains strong ⛏️
Brazilian mining company Vale said it has not observed any significant decline in global demand for minerals due to the conflict with Iran, adding that strong demand for critical minerals continues to support commodity prices and profit margins.

Key Indicators 📊

13) Oil ↓ 🛢️
Brent crude futures opened Wednesday trading at $92 per barrel, while West Texas Intermediate opened at $88 per barrel, after the US launched new strikes against Iran following the downing of a US helicopter, raising concerns about the durability of the ceasefire and prospects for a lasting peace agreement.

14) Natural Gas 🔵
US natural gas futures, as measured by the Henry Hub benchmark, opened Wednesday at $3.15 per million British thermal units (mmBtu). European gas, measured by the Dutch TTF benchmark, opened at €49 per megawatt-hour.

15) US Gasoline Prices ↑ ⛽
Average US gasoline prices rose to $4.123 per gallon on Tuesday from $4.120 a day earlier, according to GasBuddy. Before the war began, average prices stood at $2.83 per gallon.

16) Shipping ↓ 🚚
The Baltic Dirty Tanker Index fell 1.14% to 2,075 points.

Takeaways 📌

The situation continues to worsen, with growing warnings about the economic fallout and ongoing disruptions to energy and shipping markets. Despite repeated statements by Trump suggesting that an agreement to end the crisis is near, the recurrence of skirmishes and limited military strikes underscores persistent uncertainty and raises doubts about the prospects for a near-term resolution.

@RichardYabsley Petrol didn’t drop because Labor didn’t sprinkle magic commie dust over the servo. Global oil supply expectations improved, OPEC lifted output targets, refined petrol prices eased, and the local retail price cycle turned.

Why is the oil price not higher, when 20% of global output is choked off via the St of Hormuz?

Before the conflict the global oil market was already deeply in surplus.

The reason prices haven't gone even higher is because the market recognises this crisis as a logistical bottleneck, not a permanent destruction of oil reserves or infrastructure.

Its a structural oversupply capped by a logistical shock.

Post-War the market is pricing in a massive supply-and-demand double whammy

On the SUPPLY side:
Trapped Middle East barrels will flood back onto the market plus there’s also likely to be elevated output non-OPEC output remains

On the DEMAND side:
There’s accelerating structural decline via global EV & Electrification adoption

The market is currently looking past the immediate physical disruptions because it knows a wave of supply is waiting on the other side of a ceasefire, precisely at a time when global transport electrification is permanently chipping away at structural demand.

Long-term forecasting models show that once this geopolitical crisis resolves, underlying market fundamentals are heavily weighted toward an oversupply, with some estimates pointing to Brent retreating right back down toward the $60-$70 range.

Sure, everyone has biases (myself included) but the flaw in your argument here is that you're presupposing that the CEO of any given corporation could somehow afford to buy off every competing court, PDA and insurer in their region without immediately destroying its own reputation and customer base in the process.

Not to mention that agreeing to such bribery would also tank the reputations and customer bases of those courts, PDAs, and insurers — no rational actor on either side would touch it.

In a true free market, barriers to entry (regulations, subsidies, IP law, licensing) disappear. That's why markets naturally trend toward decentralization even in "hard" services like defense and infrastructure.

History shows this pattern repeatedly: private cartels and monopolies collapse the moment someone undercuts prices or offers better service — look at the railroad cartels of the 1800s or the oil cartel attempts pre-OPEC; they only lasted when government enforced them.

Defense and infrastructure face especially brutal diseconomies of scale.

Bureaucracy bloats costs, customers demand choice and exit rights, and any whiff of monopolizing violence triggers immediate backlash: clients flee to rival agencies, insurers refuse coverage for aggressors, and competing PDAs step in to enforce restitution (exactly as they do today in private arbitration and security markets).

The moment a PDA tries to act like a state, it stops being profitable because its own customers and insurers would defect.

That's why cartelization fails without the state.

Competition isn't some vague hope; it's the mechanism that punishes anyone who tries to corner the market on force.

Anarcho-capitalism doesn't oppose voluntary governance at all — PDAs can (and would) mutually agree on arbitration standards and "laws" for their own clients and properties. But that polycentric system remains non-coercive: you can always switch agencies, move to a different network, or opt out. It's the literal opposite of a state that claims a monopoly on violence over a geographic area.

Mega-corporations as we know them today (the ones with state-granted privileges, central-bank cheap credit, and regulatory moats) simply don't emerge in that environment.

Your frustration is understandable, but Austrian economics isn't dogma or a religion, it's just consistent methodology: we derive these outcomes from the logic of human action and demonstrated preference, then check history for illustration (not "proof").

But the real question still remains unanswered: without those state tools, how exactly do we get coercive mega-corporations in the first place?
